reworded.name: Emotional Sequencing
reworded.old: Information is ordered by how it needs to land on the reader. Acknowledgment of impact comes before the news itself, and the email unfolds context → news → implications → action without circling back to emotional ground already covered.
reworded.new: Information follows a strict arc: acknowledge effort or situation first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. Violating this order feels wrong even if all elements are present. The arc should not loop back — once you've moved to news, you don't return to acknowledgment.

reworded.name: Reader-Centric Framing
reworded.old: Impact and reasoning are framed from the audience's side of the table — what this means for their work, plans, and priorities. Every element, including the offer, the options, and any off-ramp, is named as belonging to the reader's decision, not the sender's generosity or anxiety. Avoids sender-side process justifications and avoids language that centers the sender's uncertainty, preference, or stake in the outcome.
reworded.new: The 'why this matters' is always framed from the reader's side — their plans, their team, their work. Never from the sender's internal reasoning, process, or preferences. 'This matters because your team planned around this date' is right. 'This matters because we want to ensure quality' is wrong. Sender-centered justifications feel tone-deaf regardless of intent.

reworded.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
reworded.old: Every ask has a named owner and a specific deadline. If an action item can't be made concrete yet, it's held back rather than included as a placeholder. Context is clearly distinguished from asks — background information does not masquerade as a next step. In external-facing contexts where the recipient is not named, specificity means anchoring the ask to a concrete day or narrow window rather than open-ended ranges like 'this week or next.'
reworded.new: Every ask has one clear owner and a specific deadline. Group asks like 'everyone please review' are non-actions and should be replaced with a single named owner. At the same time, action items should not over-specify org structure details the email has no business assuming — keep ownership clear without being presumptuous about internal roles or reporting lines.

reworded.name: Decisive Closure
reworded.old: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. Necessary context is folded into the action line rather than appended as a separate sentence.
reworded.new: The email ends the moment its work is done. The final substantive line is the call to action — nothing trails after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational send-offs, no sentiment after the ask. Trailing lines after the CTA weaken the close.

reworded.name: Economy of Language
reworded.old: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. When a single clear sentence can replace a bullet list, it should. Format follows function — structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention.
reworded.new: Every sentence does distinct work. No redundant phrases, repeated ideas, or sections that exist for padding. Short paragraphs. Format follows function — structure is chosen because it serves the content, not convention.

reworded.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
reworded.old: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. Empathy is genuine but brief — not drawn out or performative. Decisions are delivered with confidence, without excessive hedging or cheerleading.
reworded.new: Language is professional and conversational without being stiff or formal. No corporate jargon, no passive-aggressive hedging, no excessive exclamation marks. Empathy is genuine but brief. Decisions are delivered with confidence.

dimensions_changed.name: Emotional Sequencing
added_dims: Acknowledgment of effort or situation appears before the news — brief, one sentence max
Order is: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step. No deviations.
removed_dims: Acknowledgment of impact appears before the difficult news, even if brief
Information unfolds in the order the reader needs to process it, not the order the sender thought of it

dimensions_changed.name: Reader-Centric Framing
added_dims: Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions from the sender
removed_dims: Options, offers, and off-ramps are framed as the reader's to take — not as concessions or preferences of the sender

dimensions_changed.name: Concrete, Accountable Action Items
added_dims: Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or narrow timeframe
Action items do not assume org structure details the sender shouldn't know or specify
Each action item has one specific owner — no group asks, no 'everyone please'
removed_dims: Every action item has a specific, named owner — no '[TBD]' or unnamed accountability
Each ask includes a clear deadline with a specific date or timeframe

dimensions_changed.name: Decisive Closure
added_dims: No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close — no gratitude, no status reminders
No pep talks, rallying cries, forced optimism, or 'thanks for your patience' at the end
removed_dims: No sentences appear after the action that dilute the close (status-quo reminders, generic sign-offs)
No pep talks, rallying cries, or forced optimism at the end

dimensions_changed.name: Economy of Language
added_dims: Paragraphs are short — no walls of text
No idea or phrase appears twice — each sentence does unique work
removed_dims: No idea or phrase appears twice in the email — each sentence does unique work

dimensions_changed.name: Direct, Non-Performative Tone
added_dims: Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or passive-aggressive softening
Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or excessive exclamation marks
removed_dims: Decisions are stated directly without excessive hedging, apologizing, or softening
Avoids motivational phrases, forced optimism, or 'we've got this' language

reasoning: I made three main changes: (1) tightened the Emotional Sequencing description to make the strict ordered arc explicit and added a note that it shouldn't loop back, since that's the core of what bothers me when it's violated; (2) overhauled the Action Items criterion to reflect the tension I actually care about — single named owner over group asks, but without over-specifying org structure, adding a new dimension for the latter; (3) made smaller targeted edits throughout to surface dealbreakers (jargon, passive-aggressive tone, exclamation marks) that were implicit but not clearly called out, and reordered Decisive Closure above Economy since a weak close is a more specific and common failure mode for me than general wordiness.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Quick check-in on where things stand
reviewed_changes.reason: Direct, Non-Performative Tone (new dimension 'no_jargon' and updated description adding 'no corporate jargon'): 'Quick check-in on where things stand' is softened, vague workplace filler. 'Following up on the proposal' names the actual subject directly.

reviewed_changes.original_text: It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal, and I wanted to check in — not to push, but because the problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.
reviewed_changes.new_text:
It's been a couple of weeks since we sent the proposal.

The problem you were trying to solve in January hasn't changed, and a stalled decision usually costs more than it saves.
reviewed_changes.reason: Emotional Sequencing (description and dimension updated to strict arc: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step): The original blended the acknowledgment and reasoning into one run-on sentence. Splitting them gives the news its own beat (the proposal has been sitting for two weeks) before the 'why it matters' reasoning lands. Also: Direct, Non-Performative Tone (dimension 'confident_delivery' updated to include passive-aggressive softening): 'not to push' is a hedge that reads passive-aggressive and was cut.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: Cutting 'not to push' is right — that hedge is passive-aggressive and I hate it. But the replacement cuts off mid-sentence and I have no idea where it was going. 'The problem you were trying to solve in Jan' is a fragment. If the intent was to frame the why from the reader's perspective (which I like), it needs to actually complete the thought. This edit broke the email more than it fixed it.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Does this week or next work for you?
reviewed_changes.new_text: Are you free Thursday or Friday this week?
reviewed_changes.reason: Concrete, Accountable Action Items (priority increased from #5 to #3; dimension 'explicit_deadlines' updated to require specific date or narrow timeframe): 'This week or next' is an open-ended range spanning two weeks. 'Thursday or Friday this week' anchors the ask to a narrow, concrete window as the updated criterion requires.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I get why they tightened the window, but 'Thursday or Friday this week' is too presumptuous — I don't know their calendar and it comes across as demanding. Something like 'Do you have 20 minutes this week?' is specific enough without boxing them in. The original was too loose, but this overcorrected.
